content.text: Your First Task in Claude Cowork

Running your first Cowork task is different from starting a chat. In a conversation you steer every exchange; in a task you write a complete instruction, Claude executes it autonomously — reading files, calling tools, spawning subagents if needed — and returns a finished output that persists inside the Project. This guide walks through every step of that first task: creating the Project, writing a prompt that reliably produces what you want, running it, reading the activity log to understand what happened, and refining the prompt for your next run.
Task Lifecycle Overview

attrs.alt: Cowork Task Lifecycle: five stages from Create Task through Add Context, Run, Review, and Iterate or Schedule — with panels below comparing Task vs Conversation, prompt anatomy, and activity log entries

content.text: Task vs Conversation: The Core Distinction

The mental model shift that matters most when starting with Cowork: a task is a job you hand off, not a conversation you participate in. When you run a task, Claude reads the full instruction, constructs a plan, calls whatever tools it needs, and returns a complete output — without asking clarifying questions mid-run unless the prompt explicitly invites them.
Dimension
Cowork Task
Regular Conversation
Interaction model
You give the full instruction; Claude completes it autonomously
Interactive turns; you steer each step
Tool use
Automatic — Claude calls connectors, web, code execution without asking
Tool use may require your confirmation at each step
Output persistence
Saved to Project memory; accessible in future tasks
Ephemeral unless you copy it out manually
Scheduling
Can run on a cron schedule (Team/Enterprise)
Manual only
Best used for
Defined deliverables: summaries, reports, drafts, data transforms
Exploration, brainstorming, iterative design
Step 1: Create a Project

A Project is the container for a related set of tasks. It holds persistent memory (summaries of past task outputs), connector configurations, Project instructions, and the task history. Create one Project per workstream — one for a client engagement, one for a recurring reporting process, one for a research topic. Tasks inside the same Project can reference each other's outputs without you re-pasting content.
In the desktop app, click "New Project" in the Projects sidebar (left panel).
Give it a name that describes the workstream, not the first task. Good: "Q3 Customer Research". Avoid: "Summary task 2026-06-30".
Optionally add a brief description. This description is not read by Claude by default — it is for your own orientation.
Project Instructions

Project instructions are a system-level prompt that Claude reads at the start of every task in the Project. They define the standing context — who the work is for, what tone to use, what constraints always apply. Think of them as the equivalent of a CLAUDE.md file in Claude Code, but for agentic knowledge work.
Good instructions are short, factual, and constraint-focused:

# Project: Q3 Customer Research

You are assisting a B2B SaaS product team.

## Audience
- Internal: product managers and designers
- Outputs are working documents, not external-facing copy

## Constraints
- Only draw conclusions from sources explicitly provided in the task
- If a source does not contain the requested information, say so — do not infer from general knowledge
- Prefer bullet-point structure over prose for findings sections
- Cite sources inline as [filename, p.N] when quoting or paraphrasing

## Format defaults
- Headings in sentence case (not Title Case)
- Avoid em-dash heavy prose; use colons or line breaks
- Length: thorough but no filler; a 400-word output is fine if it covers the question
What not to put in Project instructions: anything that varies per task (source files, specific questions, output length for one task). That belongs in the individual task prompt.
Step 2: Write Your First Task Prompt

The task prompt is where most people under-invest on their first run. A vague prompt produces a vague output; Claude will make reasonable assumptions, but those assumptions may not match your intent. A well-constructed task prompt has four parts:
Goal
— what should Claude produce? Be specific about the deliverable, not just the subject area.
Source data
— which files, connector sources, or inline content should Claude draw from? Name them explicitly.
Format
— what structure should the output take? Markdown, JSON, a table, an email draft?
Scope guard
— what should Claude not do? A scope guard prevents hallucination on missing data and stops Claude from ranging outside the intended source material.
Example: Weak vs Strong Prompt
Weak:
Summarize the customer interviews.
This gives Claude no indication of which interviews, what to look for, or how long the summary should be. Claude will guess, and the guess may not match what you needed.
Strong:

Read the five interview transcripts attached below. For each transcript:
- Identify the top 2-3 pain points the customer described (quote the exact phrase)
- Note which product features they mentioned, if any
- Rate the customer's overall sentiment as Positive / Neutral / Negative

Then write a cross-interview synthesis (3-5 bullets) identifying the pain points
that appeared in more than one interview.

Format: a markdown document with one section per interview, followed by the synthesis.
Do not add conclusions beyond what the transcripts contain.

[Attach: interview-01.pdf through interview-05.pdf]
The strong version defines the deliverable, the source, the per-item structure, the cross-item synthesis, the format, and a scope guard ("do not add conclusions beyond what the transcripts contain").
Scope Guards in Depth

A scope guard is an explicit instruction about what Claude should not do or what it should say when information is missing. Without one, Claude will often fill gaps from its training data rather than admitting uncertainty — which produces confident-sounding but unverifiable output.
Data-bounded:
"Only use the attached files. If the answer is not in them, say 'Not found in sources.'"
Action-bounded:
"Do not send any emails or post any messages. Produce a draft for my review only."
Length-bounded:
"Write no more than 500 words per section. If you cannot cover the topic in that space, note what was omitted."
Uncertainty-bounded:
"If you are uncertain about a fact, flag it with [UNCERTAIN] rather than stating it as fact."
Step 3: Attaching Context
Before running, attach the source material the task prompt references. There are three ways to provide context:
Uploading Files Directly

Drag files into the task compose window or use the attachment icon. Supported types include PDF, DOCX, XLSX, CSV, TXT, Markdown, JSON, and common image formats. File contents are included in the task context when Claude runs.
File size limit:

The per-task attachment limit is typically 25 MB total across all files on Pro, with higher limits on Team and Enterprise. PDFs longer than ~300 pages may be truncated depending on the model's context window in use. For very large files, consider uploading to a connected Drive folder and referencing via the connector instead.
Referencing Connected Sources
If you have set up a Google Drive connector, you can type the file or folder path in the prompt and Claude will fetch it at runtime. Use natural language references:

Read the file "Q3 Customer Interviews / raw-transcripts/" from the shared Drive folder
and produce the summary described below.

Only use files in that folder. Do not read any other Drive content.

Connector access requires the Drive connector to be enabled for the Project (Settings → Connectors → Google Drive → Connect). Team and Enterprise plans can share a connector configuration across all team members within a Project.
Project Memory
Each task that completes writes a summary of its output to the Project's memory store. Future tasks can reference this without re-attaching files. In the prompt, refer to prior work by description:

Using the interview synthesis from last week's research task (stored in Project memory),
create a prioritized feature request list ordered by frequency of mention.

Add a "Source evidence" column citing the specific interviews that mentioned each request.
Claude searches Project memory automatically when the prompt references past work. You do not need to copy-paste earlier outputs.
Step 4: Running the Task

Click the "Run task" button (distinct from the conversational "Send" button — look for the play-arrow icon or the labeled button). The task enters an executing state. Unlike a conversation, you do not need to remain in the window; the task runs in the background and notifies you when complete.
What Claude Does During Execution
Plans.
Claude reads the Project instructions and task prompt, then constructs an internal execution plan. This plan is not shown to you, but its steps appear as tool-call entries in the activity log.
Reads sources.
Attached files are loaded into context. If a connector is referenced, Claude fetches the named files.
Calls tools.
For tasks that require summarization, code execution, web lookups, or writes to external systems, Claude calls the relevant tool automatically.
May spawn subagents.
For complex multi-part tasks, Claude may create child agents to handle parallel sub-tasks (for example, one subagent per interview transcript). Their progress appears in the activity log.
Produces output.
The final output is written to the task result and summarized into Project memory.
Pausing or Cancelling a Running Task

Click the stop icon next to the task while it is running. The task halts at the current tool-call boundary — not mid-sentence. Any partial output up to that point is preserved in the task result panel. If a subagent was mid-execution, it is cancelled and its partial output is discarded (the parent task output up to the pause point is kept).
Step 5: Reading the Activity Log

The activity log is one of Cowork's most valuable debugging tools. Every step Claude took during execution — reading a file, calling a tool, spawning a subagent, writing to memory — appears as an expandable entry. The log lets you answer: "Why did the output look like this?" and "Did Claude use the source I intended?"
Entry Types
Entry type
What it shows
What to look for
Task started
Timestamp, model used, Project instructions loaded
Confirm the right model and instructions were active
Connector read
File path, bytes read, pages or tokens consumed
Verify the correct file was fetched and fully read
Tool call
Tool name, inputs passed, outputs received
Diagnose unexpected tool behavior by inspecting inputs
Subagent spawned
Subagent ID, assigned sub-task
See how work was decomposed; click to expand the subagent's own log
Memory write
Summary written to Project memory
Review what was persisted for future tasks
Task completed / failed
Total duration, token count, exit reason
If failed: read the error reason before rerunning
Reading the Reasoning Trace

Expand any tool-call entry to see the full input sent to the tool and the raw output Claude received. For connector reads, this shows exactly which text was extracted from a file — useful when the output omits something you expected, because you can confirm whether the source contained it in the first place.

If Claude called a tool with parameters you did not intend (wrong file name, wrong page range), the fix is almost always in the prompt: be more explicit about the file reference, or add a scope guard preventing reads from other locations.
Step 6: Reviewing and Using the Output
The task result appears in the main panel when the task completes. It is also saved to Project memory automatically. You can:
Copy to clipboard
— paste into a document, email, or tool.
Export
— download as Markdown or plain text (PDF export on Team/Enterprise).
Reference in next task
— the output is in Project memory; just mention it in the next task prompt by description.
View history
— all past task outputs for the Project are listed in the Tasks tab, sorted by date, so you can compare runs over time.
Step 7: Iterating on the Prompt

Your first run rarely produces exactly what you want. The iteration loop is quick: read the output, read the activity log to understand why it came out that way, update the prompt, rerun. Common refinement patterns:
Output problem
Likely cause
Prompt fix
Output is too generic / not grounded in the source
No scope guard; Claude used training knowledge
Add "Only use the attached files. Say 'Not found' if absent."
Output is too long / padded
No length constraint; Claude defaulted to comprehensive
Add "Maximum 300 words per section; no introductory filler."
Wrong file was read
Ambiguous filename reference; Claude picked closest match
Reference the exact filename as it appears in Drive or the attachment
Missing a section that was requested
Task hit the context or token limit before completing
Reduce input size, or split into two tasks (summary first, then detail)
Format does not match what was requested
Project instructions conflict with task prompt on formatting
Repeat format instructions explicitly in the task prompt, not just Project instructions
End-to-End Example: First Task Walkthrough
Here is a complete first task from scratch, using a simple research summarization scenario. Follow along to understand how each step connects.
Setup
Create Project: "Competitive Analysis — CRM Tools".
Add Project instructions:

You assist a SaaS product team comparing CRM vendors.
Output must be factual and directly traceable to provided documents.
If a vendor's documentation does not address a feature, mark it as [Not documented].
Prefer tables for comparison outputs.
Task Prompt

Attached are the product documentation PDFs for three CRM tools:
- salescrm-docs.pdf
- hubspot-features.pdf
- pipedrive-overview.pdf

Create a comparison table with these columns:
| Feature | SalesCRM | HubSpot | Pipedrive |

Include these rows:
1. Contact record limit (free tier)
2. Custom field support
3. Email sequence automation
4. API rate limit (per day)
5. Mobile app availability
6. Pricing tier that includes reporting dashboards

For each cell: quote the relevant text from the source document in parentheses.
If a field is not found in the document, write [Not documented].
Do not infer from general knowledge.

After the table, write a 2-3 sentence summary of which tool appears strongest
for a 10-person sales team, based only on the information in the table.
What to Review After Running
Check the activity log: confirm three connector reads appeared (one per PDF). If only two appear, one file was not found — check the filename spelling.
Scan the table for [Not documented] cells. These signal either missing source content or a filename mismatch.
Spot-check one quote per column against the original PDF to confirm Claude extracted accurately.
If the summary draws a conclusion not supported by the table data, add to Project instructions: "The summary must only reference facts visible in the table above it."
Common First-Task Mistakes
Mistake
Why it happens
Correct approach
Writing conversational prompts ("Can you look at...?")
Habit from chat interfaces
Use imperative instructions: "Produce...", "Create...", "Summarize..."
Expecting Claude to ask for missing info mid-task
Conversational chat habit
Either provide everything upfront, or add "Ask me if unclear" (slows task)
Using the task for brainstorming or open-ended exploration
Mismatched tool for the job
Use a conversation for exploration; switch to task when you have a defined deliverable
Not checking the activity log when output is wrong
Instinct to just rerun with a tweaked prompt
Always read the log first — diagnose before fixing
Putting everything in Project instructions
Overcorrecting from forgotten context in conversations
Project instructions = standing constraints; task-specific detail belongs in the task prompt
Forgetting scope guards on sensitive tasks
Not thinking about what Claude might do autonomously
Any task that touches external systems needs explicit "Do not send / post / write" guards
When to Convert a Task to a Scheduled Task
Once you have run a task manually two or three times and the output is reliably what you need, consider scheduling it. Good candidates for scheduling:
Tasks that always read the same connector location for fresh data (weekly sales report, daily Slack digest)
Tasks where you always want the same analysis run on new content as it arrives
Tasks where the output needs to be ready at the start of your workday
Do not schedule a task until you have validated it manually. A flawed prompt scheduled to run daily will produce flawed output daily.
Summary

Your first Cowork task follows a five-step cycle: create a Project with clear instructions, write a goal-source-format-scope-guard prompt, attach the relevant context, run and observe the activity log, then iterate on the prompt based on what the log reveals. The biggest shift from conversational Claude is that tasks are complete instructions, not opening moves — Claude executes without mid-task clarification unless invited to ask. Validate a task prompt manually two or three times before scheduling it; reliable output depends on a well-scoped prompt much more than on the choice of source material.
